Research at the Molecular Sciences Institute

Computational Biology

Using systematically produced biological information to understand how the components of biological systems work as a whole to produce living organisms with complex properties and behaviors requires new theoretical and computational frameworks as well as new methods for testing predictions based on these frameworks. Thus, the Institute has created a computational biology group to work in this area. In particular, we have developed a number of simulations of living systems and are creating new methods and algorithms for handling large networks of biochemical reactions.

Instrumentation

Producing high-value biological information through observational techniques is a necessary first step in understanding living systems. This includes not only identifying mRNA, but also proteins, their modification states, protein complexes and associations, and localization at the level of the single cell and over time. Current observational techniques are not sufficient. The Institute is currently developing new instruments and techniques for studying cell membranes and receptor-ligand interactions thereon, characterizing the protein complements of cells and cell extracts, and determining intracellular states of signal transduction pathways.

Protein-Protein Interactions

Large numbers of genes work together to effect any given biological process, so that to understand biology, it is important to first understand the function of individual genes. Oftentimes, the products of genes (proteins) work by forming functional complexes or by transient interactions with other proteins. Dissecting biochemical networks via their protein-protein interactions can sometimes provide insights inaccessible using conventional genetic techniques. Thus, an important part of the work at the Institute is devoted to studying protein-protein interactions and analyzing such interaction networks.

Genomic and Evolutionary Studies

In biological systems, complexity can arise by accretion, but not by design. Research at the Institute is directed towards understanding how integrated systems can preserve their coherence through evolutionary change and the apparent over-specification and intricacy of specific subsystems with respect to their evolutionary history. Specifically, we are investigating the response of E. coli to large changes in its environment and developing analytical tools for reconstructing ancient genes, gene regulatory regions, and genomes.

New Directions

The scientific environment at the Institute is open and changing. Emerging from the forced cohabitation of scientists from diverse backgrounds, new projects and collaborations are steadily formed and, as interest dictates, pursued.
